other_observers: Norm Erthal on 6-18; Susan Allerton and Peter Derven on 6-19.; Jim Beatty, Joey Kellner, Brandon Percival, Mark Peterson, m.ob.
duration: The bird was in view for a total fo about 4 minutes on 6-18. About 25 minutes on 6-19
specific_location: 9257 Montezuma County Road G, Sheldon Zwicker Ranch, 16 miles west from US 160, GPS coordinates 12S 068801 4133231 NAD 27 CONUS (coordinates provided by Joey Kellner).
description: The field marks that I noted and called out within seconds of first seeing the bird that Norm Erthal had excitedly pointed our were these: orange crown, black throat and face, black wing with one white wing bar, all black tail! With further and calmer inspection the field marks were seen in more detail, clinching the identification and ruling out similar species and hybrids.  It was easy to see at first glance that the Hooded Oriole was quite similar in size to the four Bullock's Orioles with with it was quarreling - about 8.5 inches (22 cm). The overall shape of the bird was typical of the Oriole family, slimmer than an American Robin (Turdus migratorius) and with a longish, slender, pointed bill. With more thorough inspection the next morning, both mandibles of the bill were seen to be slightly down curved. The more slender shape of the putative Hooded Oriole became apparent, compared to the chunkier Bullock's Orioles, both perched and in flight. This gave it a different jizz, much as a Lesser Black-backed Gull (Larus fuscus) appears longer, more attenuated and less chunky than a Herring Gull (Larus argentatus).  The bird's head was relatively flat, more like that of a Bullock's Oriole or Common Grackle (Quiscalus quiscula) than the more rounded head of a Baltimore Oriole (Icterus galbula) or Orchard Oriole (Icterus spurious).  The "orange" color of the bird was really a yellow-orange compared to the rich orange of the nearby Bullock's Orioles. My notes described this color as half-way between the lemon fruit (Citrus limonia) color of Scott's Oriole and the orange fruit (Citrus sinensis) color of Bullock's. All of the yellow-orange parts of the bird appeared to be the same color, without and deeper or more faded hues. This color was more consistent with the western subspecies, I.c. nelsoni, than the Texas or eastern subspecies, I. c. sennetti.  The crown was also this yellow-orange color, as were the nape, sides, bely, rump (upper tail coverts) and vent. The tail was all black, above and below, with a slightly rounded tip. The combination of the yellow-orange vent and rump created a circumferential band of this color around the caudad part of the bird's body, separating the black upper back from the all black tail. The wings often obscured my view of the junction between the black upper back and rump, so it was not easy to say how far down the back extended caudad. I would say that the lower back was the same yellow-orange color as the rump.  Although the initial glance at the wings revealed what looked like an all black wing with the one strong, narrow, pure white wing bar, closer inspection showed that there were also subtle white edges of the primaries, secondaries and tertials. This created some subtle white "streaking" on that part of the wing, and in this respect the more distal part of the wing was not all black. I did not really see what looked like a second wing bar. I did not notice the underwing color.  The bird's throat and breast were adorned with a black shield, much wider than the beak and wide enough to be easily seen from the side, even if the bird's head was slightly turned away. This was continuous with a black mask that extended from the black lores down across the malar area and into the throat, but not posterior to the eye or above the eye. This pattern formed a right angle with the black area dropping straight down from behind the eye and straight forward toward the culmen. The cheekds and ear coverts behind the eye were yellow-orange, as was the nape. The lower edge of this breast-shield was rounded like the lower part of a police-man's badge or Greecian shield, not pointed like a Rose-breasted Grosbeak. Yellow-orange sides separated the black of the breast from the black wing.  Soft parts: The beak and eye were black. The feet were either black or dary gray.Behaviors: For the initial, shorter sighting on June 18, the bird was perched on a fence, then was briefly harassed and chased to a nearby tree by two male and two female Bullock's Orioles (Icterus bullockii). Longer observations occurred on June 19th, and the Hooded Oriole, no longer bullied by the Bullocks, was perched high in large trees, 5-10 meters up. When perched it did not move around much. It was also seen flying directly from tree to tree, about every ten to twenty minutes and at times "disapeared" into the dense foliage. I once saw it perch on the large trumpet vine hedge, but never saw it feeding, preening or doing any other behavior.Call: The Hooded Oriole was silent. If it did make any calls, they might have been drowned out by chattering Bullock's harrassing it.Plumage: Alternate, male
